reasoning:
Based on the FIDE rankings retrieved from Wikipedia right before the cutoff date, Magnus Carlsen holds the #1 ranking with a classical Elo of 2839, followed by Hikaru Nakamura at 2813. The 26-point gap is immense in classical chess; climbing past Carlsen normally requires multiple exceptional tournament performances or a significant drop by Carlsen. However, Carlsen reportedly skips classical events (such as the 2025 FIDE World Cup in Goa) and has stated intentions to step away from classical chess, meaning he will likely preserve his current rating. 
At this rate, Nakamura (or Caruana at 2795) would have to gain 26+ points in a short 3-month timeframe, which is extremely difficult at current rating levels and requires events they might not even participate in. 
As of November 16, the November FIDE rating list will still be in effect. For December and February, even if updates are made, no one is positioned to bridge this 26-point classical gap. So Carlsen will maintain his position as World No. 1. Probabilities reflect strong confidence, decreasing slightly as the forecast horizon extends to account for unforeseen events or an unexpected string of classical losses if Carlsen does play.
